ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700); no Hyderabadi-specific subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers biryani as a category; no Hyderabadi-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Hyderabadi Biryani or biryani as a food item.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Hyderabadi Biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with marinated lamb, typically served with raita and salan.

nutrient_source.title: Biryani (Mutton) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods reports mutton biryani per 100 g cooked. Hyderabadi Biryani is a regional variant of mutton biryani with comparable macronutrient profile. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton biryani entry. Minor adjustments for higher ghee and saffron content typical of Hyderabadi preparation are within the reported range.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Hyderabadi Biryani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 based on basmati rice-based mixed dishes. Basmati rice has a well-established GI of 50–58 (mean ~50) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The protein, fat, and spice content of biryani further attenuate glycemic response relative to plain rice, supporting a GI of approximately 50. GL per serving = (50 × 22.0 × 3.5) / 100 = 38.5 g. gi_category set to 'low' as GI 50 ≤ 55.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g; ~1120 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A standard 350 g serving of Hyderabadi Biryani provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments such as pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~38.5 per serving) from basmati rice carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low GI of approximately 50, the large carbohydrate load in a standard 350 g serving (~77 g carbohydrate) produces a substantial glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced portion of approximately 150–200 g and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and lamb (~3.1 g/100 g; ~10.9 g per serving) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The ghee and lamb fat in Hyderabadi Biryani contribute approximately 10.9 g saturated fat per standard serving, which approaches the daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association (≤13 g/day for a 2000 kcal diet); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 150–200 g and choose lean lamb cuts where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hyderabadi Biryani is a celebrated South Asian rice dish originating from the royal kitchens of the Nizams of Hyderabad, India. It is prepared using the kacchi (raw meat) dum method, in which bone-in lamb or mutton pieces are marinated overnight in yoghurt, saffron, fried onions, ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, and aromatic whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves. Partially cooked basmati rice is layered over the raw marinated meat in a heavy-bottomed pot, which is then sealed with dough or a tight-fitting lid and slow-cooked over a low flame (dum) for 45–90 minutes, allowing the steam to cook the meat and infuse the rice with spice and saffron fragrance. The result is a dish of distinct layers: golden saffron-tinted rice interspersed with tender, deeply spiced lamb, caramelised onions, and fresh mint and coriander. The flavour profile is complex — simultaneously aromatic, mildly spicy, slightly tangy from yoghurt, and rich from ghee. Per 100 g, Hyderabadi Biryani provides approximately 185 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 7.2 g fat (of which 3.1 g is saturated), and 22 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low), attributable to basmati rice and the fat-protein matrix that slows gastric emptying. Sodium content is moderate to high (~320 mg/100 g), primarily from salt and spice blends used in marination. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to carbohydrate load per serving, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from ghee and lamb. It is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with halal-certified lamb. In Singapore, Hyderabadi Biryani is served at Indian restaurants along Boat Quay and Little India, often accompanied by mirchi ka salan (chilli curry) and boondi raita.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Hyderabadi Biryani?
What is the glycemic index of biryani?
How is Hyderabadi Biryani different from other biryanis?
What spices are in Hyderabadi Biryani?
How much sodium is in biryani?
Is biryani good for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton biryani data, which is the closest approved primary source for this Indian regional dish. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Hyderabadi Biryani specifically exists; values are adapted from the general mutton biryani entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for mutton biryani. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for mutton biryani. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for mutton biryani. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb and ghee composition data as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this dish. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice (GI ~50, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); the protein-fat matrix of biryani is expected to attenuate glycemic response to at or below this value. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 77.0) / 100 = 38.5. Serving size of 350 g is based on typical restaurant portion for a single-serve biryani plate in Singapore. The input description contained a typographical error ('saffrom' for 'saffron') which has been corrected. Food group retained as 'Grains and staples' per input, consistent with rice-dominant composition.
